result:
I keep thinking about the way morning light climbs over the edge of my desk, confession-bright, looping gold across the half-finished crossword where 19-Across still stubbornly insists on eight letters for “imminent disappearance.” I never fill it in. I leave the space blank every time, as though the act of refusal is itself a cone of silence for all the things I have felt slipping quietly out of existence lately—credit-card-style laminated IDs for hobbies I no longer own, scattered memories of cities that have renamed half their streets, one entire language my grandfather could conjugate in his sleep but whose verbs I can’t even whistle in half-remembered dreams. There is scientific literature on how quickly languages fossilize in the mouths of the final speakers: roughly one dies every forty days. I used to underline such lines in academic journals with an aggressive fluorescent marker, as if neon green could arrest the loss. The marker has since dried up. Its nib is a bleached archipelago, and still the grammar decays, unpunctuated by my efforts. I think this is why I have begun to treat every word I lay on any surface—screen, paper, steamed-up mirror—with the complicated gentleness you show to a fledgling that fell from a nest too soon: cupped palms, open roof of the mouth, the tiniest exhale so it knows air is still being manufactured somewhere in the world.

In the current rotation the radio plays shoegaze from 1991, the year I learnt that thresholds can hum louder than anything actually passing through them. My four older cousins taught me how to stand inside the doorway of a grown-up party, chest to the jamb, invisible to anyone past the dim corridor where coats achieved critical mass on the bed. Every so often a bassline slid across the parquet, dragging trailed edges I could almost braid with my fingers. I counted those edges the way other children counted sheep. There were 108. That’s the number my nervous-system catalogue insists on, even though no neurologist has ever shown me a fitted curve between memory and music that comes out even. The same number, coincidentally, is sacred in more than one cosmology: Buddhist prayer beads, pressure points in Ayurveda, the not-yet-named moons of a planet we hadn’t discovered when I was nine. The universe is a glutton for outgrowing its partitions.

Lately I have been walking home along the detours that winter makes necessary, sidestreets rendered elbow-shaped by snowdrifts, shortcuts only dogs remember. Along one narrow lane, I watch a grey cat conduct an orchestra of icicles. He’s meticulous—tail flicking in 3/4—until a sudden gust rearranges everything and the sheet music vaporizes. He shrugs with the elegance only cats possess, then sits down to wash both forepaws as if cleanliness were a time travel device worthy of patent. I once tried to sit-dial the past with similar gestures: scrubbing the same cereal bowl on its off-white underside late at night because the design on the interior reminded me of the wallpaper in my grandmother’s kitchen. Eventually water erased the pattern. The bowl stood empty, honest, broadcasting light rather than memory. I decided at that moment that honesty is brutal only when you notice its weight. Before that, it’s merely physics.

Sometimes people ask why I collect obsolete atlases, the sort printed when countries still blamed their own outlines on imperial cartographers. They speak my name—Silas—in the tone reserved for eccentrics who keep too many grieving houseplants. What I never confess aloud is that the countries did disappear; they left in lowercase slow-motion: border fade, currency uncoupling like mismatched embroidery thread. Watching their un-selves was the first literature I ever read without an author. When you hold that atlas open on the living-room floor, knees indenting the turquoise Gulf of Bothnia, the print still smells of linseed oil and the last believer who touched it breathed out a little more 1973 than any lungs today can afford. Close the book and the world clicks shut, tidy vertebrate. You can pretend nothing has changed. I pretend all day, until groceries arrive in brown paper bags that disclose, in small sans-serif, ethical-sourcing paragraphs so earnest they sound like love letters from an intern hired to feel my guilt on corporate weekends.

It is 3:17 p.m. according to the microwave’s civic clock. Outside, the blue hour is already caching streetlamps, soaking them in indigo primer. Night begins as a monetized palette these days—someone trademarked #010B14 last month and is selling access to its wavelength under a subscription model. I read this in the same feed as headlines about biodegradable lunar rovers and permaculture yogurt. Every atom of information arrives pre-chewed, signed by influencers who won’t speak about genocide in their ancestral countries unless they can secure adequate affiliate links. At such moments I feel the old almanacs flinch within their shelf. They remember days when a comet’s arrival meant ill for king and harvest alike, royal-swaths of consequence, unhashtagged. I reach for the nearest ledger, its binding loose as piano wire, and locate the entry on the return of Halley in 1910. It still drips with apocalypse perfume. There is comfort in remembering that large-scale dread was once communal, not algorithmically portion-controlled.

Tonight the small-scale dread will be logistical: I promised to bring a dessert to Simone’s gathering. I skim the pantry and discover I am out of vanilla, borrowing hours like emotions from tomorrow. Instead I retrieve a jar of damson jam hand-labelled in ballpoint my mother pressed into the ridge before she lost her grip on cursive. The fruit was grown in a garden I can’t return to; a car park scraped it flat years ago. Yet when the lid sighs open, color finds its way to the spoon—a color more obituary than purple. I whisk it into yogurt, then fold in broken meringue, shards with the white integrity of collapsed arguments. What emerges is a provisional trifle sweet enough to make guests pause mid-bite, a half-second search behind their eyes as though the taste belongs to someone else’s retinal afterimage. I put a lid on the bowl, refrigerate the ghost. Debts paid. My phone buzzes like a worried cicada with a group-chat stream of careful enthusiasm, each exclamation mark an extra vertebra.

While it chills, I open the window by the fire escape. Frost teeters on the sill as though the ledge itself were a sentence hesitating. Someone two floors up is practicing trumpet; the notes fall slow-motion like paper snowflakes, Major 7ths resigned to minor 2nds in mid-air. I think of all the languages containing no future tense: communities who speak next week the same way one might describe a room you have not yet entered. I try it aloud: tomorrow is an unmarked doorway and the door is music. It sounds sufficiently Zen until I notice my breath condensing into little captions of itself that read like adverse-weather warnings. My body has become a pop-up reflection I can turn off by moving back inside.

Back at the counter I locate the missing word I’d left blank in the crossword. It’s “evanesce,” which of course it had to be, eight letters of vanish sewn together by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, who trafficked in opium and jotted skylarks on credit. I write it in anyway, pen digging too deep. The syllables sit there, obstinate on cheap newsprint, as if waiting for me to rescind. I don’t. For once I keep the word I encouraged to materialize. Maybe tomorrow I’ll black it out again, return it to its insomniac white. Sometimes the only mercy we have is a decent eraser.

Simone’s apartment smells of cardamom and beeswax polish. Children too young for metaphors run in circles chasing a dog that is actually a fleece blanket artfully handled by an adult. I place the bowl on the table between a plated Camembert and the evening’s centerpiece, a vase of kaleidoscopic paper roses engineered by the host’s partner to open and close via concealed thread at the exhalation threshold of candle flame. Conversation clusters around mortgages and microplastics. A lull arrives—exactly when the dessert spoon dips into damson strata. Someone says “childhood” under her breath without context. The room goes cathedral. I look at faces flickering between recognition and polite unknowing, and understand we are all carrying unsealed envelopes of vanished places. We eat them spoonful at a time, no postage, no return address.

Walking home later, emptied bowl underarm like a helmet repurposed for frivolity, I count the surviving neon of shopfronts. One says OPEN in red, another says OPEN in blue, both carrying out their own chromatic diplomacy. Between them a third sign remains dark, bulb blown, sigil in a script I do not read but whose silhouette suggests something between prayer and command. I stop, consider pressing my palm against the glass to warm the opacity. Instead I bow once, a compact genuflection. An act of courtesy for every syllable I failed to cradle properly today. Somewhere, my grandfather shifts in his astral chair and nods. The air travels through me like a vowel locating its accent. Language beginning anew begins ashore.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
